intent: Check whether the candidate can define JWT accurately, explain its three-part structure, and avoid the common misconception that signed JWT payloads are secret.
primaryQuestion: What is a JWT, and what are the three parts of a JWT?

expectedSignals.label: Defines JWT as a compact token format for carrying claims between parties, commonly used as a bearer credential in authentication and authorization flows.
Names the three dot-separated parts: header, payload, and signature.
Explains that the header usually contains token type and signing algorithm, while the payload contains claims such as sub, iat, exp, iss, aud, roles, or permissions.
Explains that the signature protects integrity and authenticity by signing the encoded header and payload with a secret or private key.
States clearly that a normal signed JWT is Base64URL-encoded, not encrypted; anyone with the token can read the header and payload.
Mentions that the server must validate signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and other expected claims before trusting the token.

redFlags: Says the JWT payload is encrypted just because it is not plain text.
Says the client needs JWT_SECRET to decode the token.
Cannot name header, payload, and signature.
Trusts decoded payload data without signature verification.
Treats JWT as a database session ID with no claims or signing semantics.

scoringHints.description: Defines JWT precisely, explains header/payload/signature, distinguishes encoding from encryption, and connects signature validation to trust.
Knows the three parts and general purpose but misses one security nuance such as issuer/audience validation or payload readability.
Can repeat that JWT is a token but cannot explain what each part does or why verification matters.
Answer shows unsafe misunderstandings about JWT secrecy, signing, or validation.

followUps.question: If you paste a JWT into a debugger and can read the payload, does that mean the token is insecure or unsigned?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate distinguishes readability from trust and integrity.

followUps.question: Which claims would you normally validate besides the signature?
followUps.purpose: Move from token structure into practical validation requirements.

en.title: JWT structure and meaning
en.displayQuestion: What is a JWT, and what are the three parts of a JWT?
en.displayIntent: Check basic JWT definition, structure, and the difference between encoding, signing, and encryption.
guidance: Start with a clear definition of JWT as a compact claims token.
Name header, payload, and signature, and explain the role of each part.
Say explicitly that signed JWT payloads are readable unless encrypted separately.
Connect token trust to signature and claim validation.
commonMistakes: Calling Base64URL encoding encryption.
Assuming decoded payload data is trustworthy before verification.
Forgetting issuer, audience, and expiration checks.
Thinking JWT_SECRET is required just to read the payload.

vi.title: Cấu trúc và ý nghĩa của JWT
vi.displayQuestion: JWT là gì? Cấu trúc 3 phần của JWT là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra định nghĩa JWT, cấu trúc cơ bản, và sự khác nhau giữa encode, ký số và mã hóa.
guidance: Bắt đầu bằng định nghĩa JWT là token nhỏ gọn dùng để truyền claims.
Nêu rõ ba phần header, payload và signature, kèm vai trò của từng phần.
Nói thẳng rằng payload của signed JWT đọc được nếu token không được mã hóa riêng.
Liên hệ việc tin token với verify signature và validate claims.
commonMistakes: Gọi Base64URL encoding là mã hóa.
Tin dữ liệu payload đã decode trước khi verify.
Quên kiểm tra issuer, audience và expiration.
Nghĩ JWT_SECRET cần thiết chỉ để đọc payload.

ja.title: JWT の構造と意味
ja.displayQuestion: JWT とは何ですか。JWT の 3 つの構成要素は何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: JWT の定義、基本構造、encoding・署名・暗号化の違いを確認する。
guidance: JWT を claims を運ぶ compact な token として定義する。
header、payload、signature を挙げ、それぞれの役割を説明する。
通常の signed JWT の payload は別途暗号化しない限り読めることを明確にする。
token を信頼するには signature と claims の検証が必要だと説明する。
commonMistakes: Base64URL encoding を暗号化と呼ぶ。
verify 前の decoded payload を信頼する。
issuer、audience、expiration の検証を忘れる。
payload を読むだけでも JWT_SECRET が必要だと考える。

sourceReferences.label: JWT.io: Introduction to JSON Web Tokens
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT structure, Base64URL sections, and validation concepts.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 7519: JSON Web Token
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT standard terminology and registered claims.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 8725: JWT Best Current Practices
sourceReferences.note: Used for security cautions around JWT validation and weak signing keys.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can compare session-based and JWT-based authentication across state, scalability, revocation, storage, and browser security trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: How are session-based authentication and JWT-based authentication different?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that session auth usually stores a random session ID in a cookie and keeps session state server-side in memory, Redis, database, or another session store.
Explains that JWT auth often sends a signed bearer token containing claims, allowing the API to validate the token without loading session state on every request.

Compares revocation: server-side sessions are easy to invalidate centrally, while stateless JWT access tokens remain usable until expiry unless a denylist, token version, or introspection check is added.
Compares scaling: sessions need shared session storage across replicas, while JWT validation is naturally easier across replicas if signing keys are shared safely.
Compares client risk: cookies raise CSRF concerns if automatically sent cross-site, while bearer tokens in JavaScript-readable storage are exposed to XSS token theft.
Notes that both approaches still require authorization checks after authentication.

redFlags: Says JWT is always stateless and therefore always better.
Says sessions cannot scale horizontally.
Thinks session cookies store the full user profile by default in express-session.
Ignores logout, revocation, CSRF, or XSS implications.
Conflates authentication with authorization.

scoringHints.description: Balances state, storage, revocation, scaling, browser attack surface, and authorization boundaries without presenting either model as universally better.
Explains the basic server-side state versus signed token difference but misses one important operational or security trade-off.
Can describe login cookies and tokens but cannot compare their consequences in production.
Answer is mostly preference or buzzwords and does not demonstrate authentication design understanding.

followUps.question: Which model makes emergency user lockout easier, and what would you add to the other model to support it?
How does the answer change for a same-origin browser app versus a mobile app calling an API?

en.title: Session auth versus JWT auth
en.displayQuestion: How are session-based authentication and JWT-based authentication different?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate whether the candidate can compare stateful sessions and signed bearer tokens in practical systems.
guidance: Compare where authentication state lives.
Discuss revocation, logout, scaling, and storage.
Cover browser-specific CSRF and XSS implications.
Clarify that authentication does not replace authorization.
commonMistakes: Saying JWT is automatically more secure.
Claiming sessions cannot scale.
Ignoring server-side invalidation needs.
Treating auth success as permission to do everything.

vi.title: Session auth so với JWT auth
vi.displayQuestion: Session-based authentication và JWT-based authentication khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng so sánh session có state phía server và bearer token đã ký trong hệ thống thực tế.
guidance: So sánh trạng thái authentication được lưu ở đâu.
Nói về revoke, logout, scaling và storage.
Bao gồm rủi ro CSRF và XSS trong browser.
Làm rõ authentication không thay thế authorization.
commonMistakes: Nói JWT tự động bảo mật hơn.
Khẳng định session không thể scale.
Bỏ qua nhu cầu invalidation phía server.
Xem auth thành công là được phép làm mọi thứ.

ja.title: session auth と JWT auth
ja.displayQuestion: session-based authentication と JWT-based authentication はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: server-side state を持つ session と signed bearer token を実務的に比較できるか確認する。
guidance: authentication state がどこにあるか比較する。
revocation、logout、scaling、storage を説明する。
browser での CSRF と XSS の影響を扱う。
authentication は authorization の代替ではないと明確にする。
commonMistakes: JWT が自動的により安全だと言う。
session は scale できないと断言する。
server-side invalidation の必要性を無視する。
auth 成功をすべての操作権限と混同する。

sourceReferences.label: Express session middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for server-side session storage and session ID cookie behavior.

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for session lifecycle, logout, and browser storage risks.

sourceReferences.label: JWT.io: Introduction to JSON Web Tokens
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT validation and bearer token structure.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can choose session or JWT based on product context, client type, revocation requirements, infrastructure, and security constraints.
primaryQuestion: When should you use sessions, and when should you use JWTs?

expectedSignals.label: Chooses sessions for traditional same-origin web apps when the server controls login, cookies, logout, idle timeout, and centralized invalidation.
Mentions sessions are a good fit when revocation, account lockout, admin kill-switches, and simple browser ergonomics matter more than stateless token distribution.
Chooses JWTs for APIs consumed by mobile apps, SPAs, multiple services, or identity provider flows where a signed portable token is useful.
Mentions JWT access tokens should usually be short-lived and paired with refresh tokens or re-authentication if long sessions are needed.
Considers infrastructure: shared session store for sessions versus key management and rotation for JWTs.
Explains that browser JWT usage often still involves cookies or a backend-for-frontend pattern to reduce XSS exposure.

redFlags: Uses JWT by default because it is modern.
Uses sessions by default without considering API clients or distributed services.
Ignores logout and forced revocation requirements.
Plans long-lived JWT access tokens for convenience.
Does not adjust the choice for browser, mobile, and machine-to-machine clients.

scoringHints.description: Chooses based on client type, trust boundary, revocation, operational complexity, security controls, and product needs.
Gives reasonable defaults but misses one key dimension such as key rotation, shared stores, or browser token storage.
Frames the decision as popularity or personal taste instead of a system design trade-off.
Answer would lead to unsafe or hard-to-operate authentication design.

followUps.question: If the product needs instant logout from all devices, how would that change your choice?
What would you choose for a same-origin admin dashboard versus a public mobile API?

en.title: Choosing sessions or JWTs
en.displayQuestion: When should you use sessions, and when should you use JWTs?
guidance: Start from client type and trust boundary.
Discuss revocation, logout, idle timeout, and operational complexity.
Compare shared session stores with JWT key management.
Mention short-lived access tokens when using JWTs.
commonMistakes: Choosing JWT because it sounds modern.
Choosing sessions without considering API and mobile clients.
Using long-lived access tokens.
Ignoring emergency revocation requirements.

vi.title: Chọn session hay JWT
vi.displayQuestion: Khi nào nên dùng session, khi nào nên dùng JWT?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá judgment kiến trúc authentication theo context.
guidance: Bắt đầu từ loại client và trust boundary.
Nói về revocation, logout, idle timeout và độ phức tạp vận hành.
So sánh shared session store với quản lý key của JWT.
Nhắc access token ngắn hạn khi dùng JWT.
commonMistakes: Chọn JWT vì nghe hiện đại.
Chọn session mà không xét API và mobile client.
Dùng access token sống quá lâu.
Bỏ qua yêu cầu revoke khẩn cấp.

ja.title: session と JWT の選択
ja.displayQuestion: どのような場合に session を使い、どのような場合に JWT を使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: context に応じた authentication architecture の判断力を評価する。
guidance: client type と trust boundary から始める。
revocation、logout、idle timeout、運用上の複雑さを説明する。
shared session store と JWT key management を比較する。
JWT を使う場合は short-lived access token に触れる。
commonMistakes: 現代的に聞こえるから JWT を選ぶ。
API や mobile client を考えず session を選ぶ。
長寿命の access token を使う。
緊急 revocation 要件を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for session lifecycle, timeouts, logout, and storage considerations.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 6749: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework
sourceReferences.note: Used for access token and refresh token concepts in distributed authorization flows.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 8725: JWT Best Current Practices
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT security design and key strength considerations.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can design the full Express JWT authentication flow from login to protected routes, refresh, logout, and error handling.
primaryQuestion: How would you implement JWT authentication in Express? Walk through the full flow.

expectedSignals.label: Starts with user registration or login, validates input, fetches the user, and verifies the password with a slow password hashing algorithm such as bcrypt or Argon2.
Issues a short-lived access token with minimal claims such as sub, role/scope, iat, exp, iss, and aud, using a strong secret or asymmetric key.
Optionally issues a longer-lived refresh token stored server-side as a hashed token, token family, jti, or rotation record so it can be revoked.
Chooses token transport intentionally: Authorization Bearer header, HttpOnly Secure SameSite cookie, or a backend-for-frontend pattern depending on the client.
Adds authentication middleware that extracts the token, verifies signature and claims, loads or checks the user if needed, attaches a minimal req.user, and calls next.
Uses protected routes with auth middleware before route handlers and separate authorization middleware for roles, scopes, or resource ownership.
Implements a refresh endpoint that validates the refresh token, rotates it, issues a new access token, and rejects reuse or revoked tokens.
Implements logout by clearing client storage and revoking refresh tokens or token families; accepts that existing stateless access tokens live until expiry.
Handles errors consistently with 401 for missing/invalid/expired credentials and 403 for authenticated users lacking permission.
Mentions HTTPS, rate limiting login/refresh, safe logging, secret management, CORS/credentials configuration, and tests for middleware failures.

redFlags: Stores plain-text passwords or compares passwords directly.
Puts password, credit card, or large profile data into the JWT payload.
Uses jwt.decode instead of jwt.verify in middleware.
Creates long-lived access tokens and no revocation path.
Parses JWT manually in every controller instead of middleware.
Uses a hardcoded weak secret in source code.

scoringHints.description: Covers login, signing, token transport, middleware verification, req.user, authorization, refresh rotation, logout/revocation, errors, and production hardening.
Can implement login and protected routes but misses one production detail such as refresh rotation, revocation, storage, or error semantics.
Knows jwt.sign and jwt.verify but cannot describe a complete and safe authentication lifecycle.
Flow contains unsafe choices that would expose users or make logout and revocation impossible to reason about.

followUps.question: Where exactly would you put the auth middleware and what shape would req.user have after it succeeds?
followUps.purpose: Check Express implementation detail beyond high-level token talk.

followUps.question: How would your flow change if the token is stored in an HttpOnly cookie instead of sent in an Authorization header?
followUps.purpose: Probe transport-specific CSRF, CORS, and credentials handling.

en.title: Full Express JWT auth flow
en.displayQuestion: How would you implement JWT authentication in Express? Walk through the full flow.
en.displayIntent: Evaluate complete practical implementation from login to protected routes, refresh, logout, and production hardening.
guidance: Walk through login, password verification, token signing, and client transport.
Explain JWT middleware, req.user, route protection, and authorization separation.
Include refresh token rotation and logout/revocation strategy.
Mention HTTPS, rate limiting, secret management, and consistent errors.
commonMistakes: Using decode instead of verify.
Putting sensitive payloads in tokens.
Skipping refresh token revocation.
Mixing authentication and authorization in every route handler.

vi.title: Flow JWT auth đầy đủ trong Express
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để implement JWT authentication trong Express? Hãy đi qua flow đầy đủ.
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng implement thực tế từ login, protected route, refresh, logout đến hardening production.
guidance: Đi qua login, verify password, ký token và cách gửi/lưu token phía client.
Giải thích JWT middleware, req.user, bảo vệ route và tách authorization.
Bao gồm refresh token rotation và chiến lược logout/revocation.
Nhắc HTTPS, rate limiting, quản lý secret và error nhất quán.
commonMistakes: Dùng decode thay vì verify.
Đưa dữ liệu nhạy cảm vào token.
Bỏ qua revoke refresh token.
Trộn authentication và authorization trong từng route handler.

ja.title: Express の JWT auth flow 全体
ja.displayQuestion: Express で JWT authentication をどのように実装しますか。全体の flow を説明してください。
ja.displayIntent: login、protected route、refresh、logout、production hardening まで実装全体を評価する。
guidance: login、password verification、token signing、client transport を順に説明する。
JWT middleware、req.user、route protection、authorization の分離を説明する。
refresh token rotation と logout/revocation strategy を含める。
HTTPS、rate limiting、secret management、一貫した errors に触れる。
commonMistakes: verify ではなく decode を使う。
sensitive data を token に入れる。
refresh token revocation を省略する。
各 route handler に authentication と authorization を混ぜる。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware ordering and route protection patterns.

sourceReferences.label: jsonwebtoken package README
sourceReferences.note: Used for jwt.sign, jwt.verify, jwt.decode, exp, and error behavior.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 6749: Refreshing an Access Token
sourceReferences.note: Used for refresh token flow and rotation context.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands the different purposes, lifetimes, storage, and revocation characteristics of access tokens and refresh tokens.
primaryQuestion: How are access tokens and refresh tokens different in purpose and lifetime?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that an access token is presented to resource APIs to access protected endpoints.
Explains that a refresh token is presented only to the authorization server or refresh endpoint to obtain a new access token.
States that access tokens should be short-lived to reduce the damage of theft.
States that refresh tokens are usually longer-lived, more sensitive, and must be stored and protected more carefully.
Mentions refresh token rotation, server-side storage, hashing, token family tracking, or reuse detection as practical controls.
Mentions that refresh tokens should not be sent to every API request.
Explains that scopes/permissions on access tokens should be limited to what the resource server needs.

redFlags: Uses one long-lived JWT as both access and refresh token.
Sends refresh tokens on every API request.
Stores refresh tokens without any revocation or rotation path.
Thinks refresh tokens are safer because they are used less often and therefore do not need protection.
Cannot explain why access tokens should expire quickly.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates API access from token renewal, explains lifetime differences, storage sensitivity, rotation, revocation, and request usage.
Understands short-lived access and longer-lived refresh tokens but misses one operational control such as rotation or reuse detection.
Knows the terms but cannot explain why two token types are used.
Design would expose long-lived credentials or provide no meaningful revocation path.

followUps.question: Which token should your normal GET /me request send, and which endpoint should receive the refresh token?
What is refresh token rotation trying to detect or prevent?

en.title: Access token versus refresh token
en.displayQuestion: How are access tokens and refresh tokens different in purpose and lifetime?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands token roles, lifetimes, and revocation controls.
guidance: Separate protected-resource access from token renewal.
Explain why access tokens are short-lived and refresh tokens are more sensitive.
Mention rotation, hashing, server-side storage, and reuse detection.
Clarify that refresh tokens should not be sent to normal resource APIs.
commonMistakes: Using one long-lived token for everything.
Sending refresh tokens with every request.
Skipping rotation or revocation.
Treating refresh tokens as low-risk because they are used less frequently.

vi.title: Access token so với refresh token
vi.displayQuestion: Access token và refresh token khác nhau về mục đích và thời hạn như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về vai trò token, thời hạn sống và cơ chế revocation.
guidance: Tách truy cập protected resource khỏi việc xin token mới.
Giải thích vì sao access token ngắn hạn còn refresh token nhạy cảm hơn.
Nhắc rotation, hashing, lưu phía server và phát hiện reuse.
Làm rõ refresh token không nên gửi tới API resource thông thường.
commonMistakes: Dùng một token sống lâu cho mọi mục đích.
Gửi refresh token trong mọi request.
Bỏ qua rotation hoặc revocation.
Xem refresh token ít rủi ro vì dùng ít hơn.

ja.title: access token と refresh token
ja.displayQuestion: access token と refresh token は目的と寿命においてどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: token の役割、寿命、revocation control の理解を確認する。
guidance: protected resource access と token renewal を分けて説明する。
access token が短命で refresh token がより sensitive である理由を説明する。
rotation、hashing、server-side storage、reuse detection に触れる。
refresh token を通常の resource API に送らないことを明確にする。
commonMistakes: 1 つの長寿命 token をすべてに使う。
refresh token を毎 request で送る。
rotation や revocation を省略する。
使用頻度が低いから refresh token は低リスクだと考える。

sourceReferences.label: RFC 6749: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework
sourceReferences.note: Used for access token and refresh token definitions and refresh flow.

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for token/session lifecycle and invalidation principles.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can choose browser token storage with a realistic understanding of localStorage, HttpOnly cookies, XSS, CSRF, CORS, and token lifetimes.
primaryQuestion: Where should a JWT be stored on the client: localStorage or an HttpOnly cookie? Why?

expectedSignals.label: States that localStorage is accessible to JavaScript, so an XSS bug can read and exfiltrate tokens directly.
Explains that an HttpOnly cookie cannot be read by JavaScript, reducing direct token theft through XSS.
Explains that cookies are sent automatically by the browser, so CSRF and cross-site request behavior must be controlled with SameSite, CSRF tokens, origin checks, CORS credentials, and Secure.

Mentions that for browser apps, a common safer pattern is short-lived access tokens in memory plus a refresh token in an HttpOnly Secure SameSite cookie, or a backend-for-frontend that keeps tokens off the browser entirely.
Mentions localStorage may be simpler for pure bearer-token APIs but persists across browser restarts and is not a strong storage boundary against XSS.
Explains that whichever storage is chosen, XSS prevention, CSP, dependency hygiene, and token lifetime remain necessary.

redFlags: Says localStorage is secure because it is same-origin.
Says HttpOnly cookies solve all XSS problems.
Ignores CSRF when using cookies.
Stores long-lived refresh tokens in localStorage for convenience.
Uses SameSite=None without Secure in production browser flows.

scoringHints.description: Compares XSS and CSRF trade-offs accurately, recommends cookie/BFF or memory-plus-cookie patterns where appropriate, and includes browser hardening details.
Prefers HttpOnly cookies and explains XSS risk but under-explains CSRF or CORS implications.
Knows localStorage and cookies exist but cannot reason about browser attack models.
Answer would store long-lived sensitive tokens in a way that is easy to steal or replay.

followUps.question: If you move a JWT from localStorage to an HttpOnly cookie, what new CSRF controls do you need?
followUps.purpose: Check whether the candidate understands the cookie trade-off.

followUps.question: What would you store in memory, what would you store in a cookie, and what would you never store in the browser?

en.title: JWT storage in browsers
en.displayQuestion: Where should a JWT be stored on the client: localStorage or an HttpOnly cookie? Why?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate browser token storage judgment across XSS, CSRF, persistence, and transport behavior.
guidance: Compare JavaScript-readable storage with HttpOnly cookies.
Discuss XSS token theft and CSRF request forgery separately.
Mention Secure, SameSite, CSRF tokens, origin checks, and CORS credentials.
Describe practical patterns such as BFF or refresh cookie plus in-memory access token.
commonMistakes: Calling localStorage safe because of same-origin.
Ignoring CSRF with cookies.
Keeping long-lived refresh tokens in localStorage.
Thinking HttpOnly prevents all XSS impact.

vi.title: Lưu JWT trong browser
vi.displayQuestion: JWT nên được lưu ở đâu phía client: localStorage hay HttpOnly cookie? Tại sao?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá judgment lưu token trong browser qua XSS, CSRF, persistence và hành vi transport.
guidance: So sánh storage JavaScript đọc được với HttpOnly cookie.
Tách rủi ro XSS token theft khỏi CSRF request forgery.
Nhắc Secure, SameSite, CSRF token, origin check và CORS credentials.
Mô tả pattern thực tế như BFF hoặc refresh cookie cộng access token trong memory.
commonMistakes: Gọi localStorage là an toàn vì same-origin.
Bỏ qua CSRF khi dùng cookie.
Giữ refresh token sống lâu trong localStorage.
Nghĩ HttpOnly ngăn được mọi tác động của XSS.

ja.title: browser での JWT storage
ja.displayQuestion: client 側で JWT は localStorage と HttpOnly cookie のどちらに保存すべきですか。なぜですか。
ja.displayIntent: XSS、CSRF、persistence、transport behavior を踏まえた browser token storage の判断力を評価する。
guidance: JavaScript-readable storage と HttpOnly cookie を比較する。
XSS token theft と CSRF request forgery を分けて説明する。
Secure、SameSite、CSRF token、origin check、CORS credentials に触れる。
BFF や refresh cookie と in-memory access token の pattern を説明する。
commonMistakes: same-origin だから localStorage は安全だと言う。
cookie 利用時の CSRF を無視する。
長寿命 refresh token を localStorage に置く。
HttpOnly が XSS の影響をすべて防ぐと考える。

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for browser storage and session token handling cautions.

sourceReferences.label: MDN: Secure cookie configuration
sourceReferences.note: Used for Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, and cookie prefix guidance.

sourceReferences.label: OWASP JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT storage risk framing, with language-independent security principles.

intent: Check whether the candidate can protect Express routes with JWT middleware using correct extraction, verification, request augmentation, and error semantics.
primaryQuestion: How do you protect a route with JWT middleware in Express?

expectedSignals.label: Registers middleware before protected routes, either globally for an authenticated router or route-by-route for specific endpoints.
Extracts the token from the Authorization: Bearer header or from a configured cookie, with a clear rule for missing or malformed tokens.
Uses jwt.verify with the correct secret/public key and validates expected claims such as exp, issuer, audience, and algorithm.
Attaches a minimal trusted identity to req.user, such as userId, roles, scopes, or tokenId, rather than passing the raw decoded payload everywhere.
Returns 401 for missing, invalid, or expired tokens and calls next only after successful verification.
Keeps authorization separate by adding role/scope/ownership middleware after authentication where needed.
Avoids logging full tokens and avoids leaking detailed verification errors to clients.

redFlags: Uses jwt.decode and treats the result as authenticated.
Accepts tokens from arbitrary query strings without a strong reason.
Calls next after sending a 401 response.
Returns 403 for missing credentials and 401 for permissions backwards without explanation.
Lets every route handler re-parse tokens manually.

scoringHints.description: Describes route registration, Bearer/cookie extraction, verification, req.user shaping, 401 handling, next control flow, and authz separation.
Can implement basic verification middleware but misses one detail such as claims, error semantics, or req.user shape.
Knows there is middleware but cannot safely implement token extraction and control flow.
Middleware would accept unverified tokens or produce inconsistent and unsafe route behavior.

followUps.question: Show the control flow for missing token, invalid signature, expired token, and successful token.
What data would you put on req.user, and what data would you load later from the database?
followUps.purpose: Check trust boundaries and request augmentation.

en.title: Protecting Express routes with JWT
en.displayQuestion: How do you protect a route with JWT middleware in Express?
en.displayIntent: Check practical JWT middleware implementation and route protection semantics.
guidance: Explain where the middleware is registered.
Extract the token consistently from Bearer header or cookie.
Use verify, attach a minimal req.user, and call next only on success.
Separate authentication from role, scope, or ownership authorization.
commonMistakes: Trusting jwt.decode.
Calling next after sending an error.
Putting auth logic in every controller.
Leaking full token values in logs.

vi.title: Bảo vệ route Express bằng JWT
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để bảo vệ một route bằng JWT middleware trong Express?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng implement JWT middleware thực tế và semantics khi bảo vệ route.
guidance: Giải thích middleware được đăng ký ở đâu.
Lấy token nhất quán từ Bearer header hoặc cookie.
Dùng verify, gắn req.user tối thiểu và chỉ gọi next khi thành công.
Tách authentication khỏi authorization theo role, scope hoặc ownership.
commonMistakes: Tin jwt.decode.
Gọi next sau khi đã gửi lỗi.
Đưa auth logic vào mọi controller.
Log toàn bộ token.

ja.title: JWT で Express route を保護する
ja.displayQuestion: Express で JWT middleware を使って route をどう保護しますか。
ja.displayIntent: JWT middleware の実装と route protection semantics を確認する。
guidance: middleware をどこに登録するか説明する。
Bearer header または cookie から一貫して token を抽出する。
verify を使い、最小限の req.user を付け、成功時だけ next を呼ぶ。
authentication と role/scope/ownership authorization を分離する。
commonMistakes: jwt.decode を信頼する。
error response 後に next を呼ぶ。
すべての controller に auth logic を置く。
token 全体を log に出す。

sourceReferences.label: Express docs: Using middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware composition and route-level middleware behavior.

sourceReferences.label: jsonwebtoken package README
sourceReferences.note: Used for jwt.verify and token verification behavior.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 6750: Bearer Token Usage
sourceReferences.note: Used for Authorization Bearer token transport terminology.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands the difference between creating, validating, and merely reading JWTs in common Node.js JWT libraries.
primaryQuestion: How are jwt.sign(), jwt.verify(), and jwt.decode() different?

expectedSignals.label: Explains jwt.sign creates a token from a payload and signing key, usually with options such as expiresIn, issuer, audience, subject, algorithm, or key id.
Explains jwt.verify validates the token signature and relevant registered claims, and returns a decoded payload only if verification succeeds.
Explains jwt.decode parses the token without validating signature, expiration, issuer, or audience, so it must not be used for authentication decisions.
Mentions that verify can fail with errors such as invalid signature, malformed token, not-before, or expired token.
Mentions that the algorithm and key selection must be controlled by server configuration, not blindly trusted from token input.
Uses decode only for non-security tasks such as debugging or reading a kid/header before selecting a verification key, with care.

redFlags: Says decode verifies the token.
Uses decode in auth middleware because it is faster.
Assumes verify only checks expiration and not signature.
Lets the token choose any algorithm or key without server-side constraints.
Signs tokens with client-provided payloads without controlling sensitive claims.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates creation, verified validation, and untrusted parsing, including claim checks, errors, and algorithm/key control.
Understands sign and verify but under-explains decode risk or claim validation.
Can name the functions but does not know which one is safe for authentication.
Answer would allow unverified token data to control protected routes.

followUps.question: If jwt.decode returns a userId and role, why is that not enough to set req.user?
followUps.purpose: Correct the common unsafe decode misconception.

followUps.question: Which options would you pass to verify in production besides the key?
followUps.purpose: Check issuer, audience, algorithm, and clock tolerance awareness.

en.title: sign, verify, and decode
en.displayQuestion: How are jwt.sign(), jwt.verify(), and jwt.decode() different?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate knows which JWT operation creates, validates, or merely parses a token.
guidance: Define sign as token creation with claims and a signing key.
Define verify as signature and claim validation.
Define decode as untrusted parsing only.
Mention errors and server-controlled algorithm/key selection.
commonMistakes: Using decode for authentication.
Forgetting expiration, issuer, and audience checks.
Trusting token-selected algorithms blindly.
Letting clients control sensitive claims at signing time.

vi.title: sign, verify và decode
vi.displayQuestion: jwt.sign(), jwt.verify(), jwt.decode() khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên biết thao tác JWT nào tạo token, thao tác nào validate, và thao tác nào chỉ parse.
guidance: Định nghĩa sign là tạo token với claims và signing key.
Định nghĩa verify là validate signature và claims.
Định nghĩa decode chỉ là parse dữ liệu chưa đáng tin.
Nhắc lỗi verify và việc server phải kiểm soát algorithm/key.
commonMistakes: Dùng decode để authentication.
Quên kiểm tra expiration, issuer và audience.
Tin algorithm do token đưa ra một cách mù quáng.
Cho client kiểm soát sensitive claims lúc sign.

ja.title: sign、verify、decode
ja.displayQuestion: jwt.sign(), jwt.verify(), jwt.decode() はどう違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: JWT operation のうち、作成・検証・単なる parse の違いを理解しているか確認する。
guidance: sign は claims と signing key で token を作ることだと定義する。
verify は signature と claims を検証することだと定義する。
decode は信頼できない parse にすぎないと定義する。
verify errors と server-controlled algorithm/key selection に触れる。
commonMistakes: authentication に decode を使う。
expiration、issuer、audience checks を忘れる。
token が示す algorithm を盲目的に信頼する。
sign 時に client が sensitive claims を制御できるようにする。

sourceReferences.label: jsonwebtoken package README
sourceReferences.note: Used for sign, verify, decode, claims, options, and error behavior.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 8725: JWT Best Current Practices
sourceReferences.note: Used for algorithm and key-management security cautions.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can handle expired JWTs correctly on both API and client sides without unsafe retries or silent failures.
primaryQuestion: What happens when a JWT is expired? How should the client handle it?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that jwt.verify should reject an expired token based on the exp claim and the server should not authorize the request.
States that the API should normally return 401 with a consistent error code such as token_expired or invalid_token, without exposing sensitive internals.
Explains that the client can call a refresh endpoint if it has a valid refresh token, then retry the original request once with the new access token.
Explains that if refresh fails, the client should clear local auth state and send the user through login again.
Mentions avoiding infinite refresh loops, coordinating concurrent refresh requests, and handling clock skew carefully.
Mentions proactive refresh shortly before expiry as an optional UX improvement, while still relying on server validation.

redFlags: Allows the client to extend exp locally.
Treats an expired access token as still valid if the user appears logged in on the frontend.
Retries refresh forever on every 401.
Uses jwt.decode on the client as the source of truth for authorization.
Responds with 500 for normal token expiry.

scoringHints.description: Explains server rejection, 401 semantics, refresh once, retry once, logout on refresh failure, concurrency control, and clock-skew nuance.
Understands expiry and refresh but misses one client-side reliability detail such as infinite loop prevention or concurrent refresh.
Knows expired tokens fail but cannot design clean client/server handling.
Answer permits expired tokens or creates brittle retry behavior.

followUps.question: What should happen if five API calls all get 401 because the same access token expired?
followUps.purpose: Probe refresh request deduplication and retry behavior.

followUps.question: Can the frontend safely edit exp or generate a new access token by itself?

en.title: Expired JWT handling
en.displayQuestion: What happens when a JWT is expired? How should the client handle it?
en.displayIntent: Check server-side expiry enforcement and robust client refresh behavior.
guidance: Explain server verification failure and 401 response.
Describe refresh, retry once, and logout on refresh failure.
Mention infinite loop prevention and concurrent refresh coordination.
Do not rely on client-side decode as authority.
commonMistakes: Letting the client extend expiration.
Returning 500 for token expiry.
Retrying refresh forever.
Trusting frontend auth state over server validation.

vi.title: Xử lý JWT hết hạn
vi.displayQuestion: Điều gì xảy ra khi JWT bị expired? Client xử lý thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra việc enforce expiry phía server và xử lý refresh ổn định phía client.
guidance: Giải thích verify fail phía server và trả 401.
Mô tả refresh, retry một lần và logout khi refresh fail.
Nhắc tránh infinite loop và điều phối concurrent refresh.
Không xem decode phía client là nguồn quyền lực.
commonMistakes: Cho client tự kéo dài expiration.
Trả 500 cho token expiry.
Retry refresh vô hạn.
Tin frontend auth state hơn server validation.

ja.title: expired JWT の扱い
ja.displayQuestion: JWT が expired になると何が起きますか。client はどう処理すべきですか。
ja.displayIntent: server-side expiry enforcement と client refresh behavior を確認する。
guidance: server verification failure と 401 response を説明する。
refresh、1 回だけ retry、refresh failure 時の logout を説明する。
infinite loop prevention と concurrent refresh coordination に触れる。
client-side decode を権威として扱わない。
commonMistakes: client に expiration を延長させる。
token expiry に 500 を返す。
refresh を無限 retry する。
frontend auth state を server validation より信頼する。

sourceReferences.label: jsonwebtoken package README
sourceReferences.note: Used for exp, TokenExpiredError, and verify behavior.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 7519: exp claim
sourceReferences.note: Used for the expiration time claim semantics.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 6749: Invalid Token and Refresh Flow
sourceReferences.note: Used for invalid access token and refresh-token flow context.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands what express-session does, where session data is stored, and what production configuration is required.
primaryQuestion: What is express-session and what is it used for?

expectedSignals.label: Defines express-session as Express middleware for creating and managing server-side login sessions or other per-user session state.
Explains that the browser receives a session ID cookie, while the session data itself is stored server-side by the configured store.
Mentions req.session as the request object used to read and mutate session data.
Mentions core options such as secret, name, resave, saveUninitialized, rolling, cookie.maxAge, httpOnly, secure, and sameSite.
Warns that the default MemoryStore is for development/debugging and should not be used in production because it does not scale and can leak memory.
Mentions production stores such as Redis, database-backed stores, or managed stores for multi-instance deployments.
Mentions regenerating session IDs after login or privilege changes to reduce session fixation risk, and destroying sessions on logout.

redFlags: Thinks express-session stores the entire session data in the browser cookie by default.
Uses MemoryStore in production.
Does not set a strong secret or secure cookie flags.
Stores passwords, tokens, or large sensitive objects directly in req.session.
Cannot explain how logout destroys server-side session state.

scoringHints.description: Explains session ID cookie, server-side store, req.session, safe options, production store, session regeneration, and logout destruction.
Understands basic session middleware but misses one production detail such as store choice, secure cookie flags, or fixation prevention.
Knows express-session exists but cannot explain where data lives or how it scales.
Answer would lead to insecure or non-scalable session management.

followUps.question: What happens to user sessions if you run three Node processes with the default MemoryStore?
What should logout do to the server-side session and the browser cookie?

en.title: express-session purpose
en.displayQuestion: What is express-session and what is it used for?
en.displayIntent: Check Express server-side session fundamentals and production configuration judgment.
guidance: Explain session ID cookie versus server-side session data.
Mention req.session and common options.
Call out MemoryStore as development-only.
Discuss production stores, secure cookie flags, regeneration, and logout destroy.
commonMistakes: Thinking all session data is in the cookie.
Using MemoryStore in production.
Forgetting secure cookie options.
Not destroying server state on logout.

vi.title: Mục đích của express-session
vi.displayQuestion: express-session là gì và dùng để làm gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra nền tảng session phía server trong Express và judgment cấu hình production.
guidance: Giải thích session ID cookie so với session data phía server.
Nhắc req.session và các option phổ biến.
Nêu rõ MemoryStore chỉ dùng development.
Nói về production store, secure cookie flags, regenerate và destroy khi logout.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ toàn bộ session data nằm trong cookie.
Dùng MemoryStore ở production.
Quên option cookie bảo mật.
Không destroy server state khi logout.

ja.title: express-session の目的
ja.displayQuestion: express-session とは何で、何に使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: Express の server-side session 基礎と production configuration の判断力を確認する。
guidance: session ID cookie と server-side session data の違いを説明する。
req.session と一般的な options に触れる。
MemoryStore は development-only だと明確にする。
production store、secure cookie flags、regeneration、logout destroy を説明する。
commonMistakes: session data がすべて cookie に入ると考える。
production で MemoryStore を使う。
secure cookie options を忘れる。
logout 時に server state を destroy しない。

sourceReferences.label: Express session middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for express-session behavior, server-side storage, options, and MemoryStore production warning.

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for session lifecycle, logout, timeout, and invalidation guidance.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands what cookie-parser does and does not do in an Express application.

expectedSignals.label: Defines cookie-parser as middleware that parses the Cookie header and populates req.cookies.
Explains that when configured with a secret, it can validate signed cookies and expose them in req.signedCookies.
Explains signed cookies provide tamper detection, not encryption or confidentiality.
Mentions JSON cookies with j: prefix may be parsed into values, but cookie values are still client-controlled input.
Distinguishes cookie-parser from body parsers such as express.json and from express-session.
Mentions that recent express-session versions do not require cookie-parser and using different secrets can cause issues.
Mentions that setting cookies is done through response headers or res.cookie; cookie-parser mainly reads incoming cookies.

redFlags: Says cookie-parser encrypts cookies.
Uses signed cookies as a safe place for secrets.
Confuses req.cookies with req.body.
Assumes cookie-parser creates sessions by itself.
Trusts cookie values without validation.

scoringHints.description: Explains Cookie header parsing, req.cookies, signed cookies, tamper detection, JSON cookies, differences from sessions/body parsing, and validation needs.
Understands req.cookies but misses one nuance such as signed cookies not being encrypted or express-session interaction.
Can install cookie-parser but cannot explain its security properties or boundaries.
Answer would misuse cookies for sensitive data or confuse request data sources.

followUps.question: If a cookie is signed, can the user still read its value in the browser?
followUps.purpose: Check distinction between signing and encryption.

followUps.question: Why does express-session not normally need cookie-parser anymore?
followUps.purpose: Probe integration detail and avoid unnecessary middleware assumptions.

en.title: cookie-parser purpose
en.displayIntent: Check understanding of Express cookie parsing, signed cookies, and middleware boundaries.
guidance: Explain req.cookies and req.signedCookies.
Clarify that signing detects tampering but does not encrypt.
Distinguish cookies from body parsing and sessions.
Treat cookie values as untrusted client input.
commonMistakes: Calling signed cookies encrypted.
Putting secrets in client-visible cookies.
Confusing req.cookies with req.body.
Thinking cookie-parser creates sessions.

vi.title: Mục đích của cookie-parser
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về parse cookie trong Express, signed cookie và ranh giới middleware.
guidance: Giải thích req.cookies và req.signedCookies.
Làm rõ signing giúp phát hiện sửa đổi nhưng không mã hóa.
Phân biệt cookie với body parsing và session.
Xem cookie value là input không đáng tin từ client.
commonMistakes: Gọi signed cookie là encrypted.
Đưa secret vào cookie client có thể thấy.
Nhầm req.cookies với req.body.
Nghĩ cookie-parser tạo session.

ja.title: cookie-parser の目的
ja.displayQuestion: cookie-parser は何に使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: Express の cookie parsing、signed cookie、middleware boundary の理解を確認する。
guidance: req.cookies と req.signedCookies を説明する。
signing は tampering 検出であり encryption ではないと明確にする。
cookie、body parsing、session を区別する。
cookie values は untrusted client input として扱う。
commonMistakes: signed cookie を encrypted と呼ぶ。
client-visible cookie に secret を入れる。
req.cookies と req.body を混同する。
cookie-parser が session を作ると考える。

sourceReferences.label: Express cookie-parser middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for req.cookies, req.signedCookies, signed cookies, and JSON cookies.

sourceReferences.label: Express session middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for the note that express-session no longer requires cookie-parser and reads/writes cookies directly.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands JWT payload visibility, data minimization, stale claims, and why sensitive data must not be stored in tokens.
primaryQuestion: Why should you not store sensitive information such as passwords or credit cards in a JWT payload?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that a normal signed JWT payload is Base64URL-encoded and readable by anyone who has the token.
Explains that signing protects integrity, not confidentiality; encryption would require a different design such as JWE and still needs careful key management.
Mentions tokens can appear in browser storage, logs, monitoring tools, referrers, support screenshots, crash reports, or network traces.
Applies data minimization: include only claims needed by the API, such as subject, roles/scopes, issuer, audience, expiration, and token ID.
Mentions sensitive or frequently changing data should be fetched from the database or authorization service after authentication.
Explains stale-claim risk: if roles or account status change, long-lived JWT payloads may remain outdated until expiry or revocation.
Mentions compliance and incident impact for passwords, credit cards, secrets, PII, and session credentials.

redFlags: Says JWT payload is safe because the token is signed.
Stores password hash, credit card, API keys, or refresh token values in access token claims.
Treats roles in a long-lived token as permanently trustworthy after account changes.
Ignores log and telemetry leakage.
Adds large user profile objects to reduce database queries without considering privacy or staleness.

scoringHints.description: Explains readability, signing versus encryption, data minimization, leakage surfaces, stale claims, and compliance impact.
Knows JWT payloads are readable but misses one nuance such as stale authorization data or logging exposure.
Avoids sensitive data by rule of thumb but cannot explain the technical reason.
Answer would put high-impact secrets or regulated data in client-visible tokens.

followUps.question: Which fields would you include in an access token for a normal user API, and which fields would you load from the database?
followUps.purpose: Probe practical claim design and data minimization.

followUps.question: If the JWT is signed with HS256, who can read the payload?
followUps.purpose: Correct confusion between signing and confidentiality.

en.title: Sensitive data in JWT payloads
en.displayQuestion: Why should you not store sensitive information such as passwords or credit cards in a JWT payload?
en.displayIntent: Check JWT payload visibility, data minimization, and stale-claim risk.
guidance: Explain that signed JWT payloads are readable.
Separate integrity from confidentiality.
Discuss logs, browser storage, telemetry, and screenshots as leakage surfaces.
Recommend minimal claims and server-side lookup for sensitive or changing data.
commonMistakes: Thinking signed means secret.
Putting password hashes or payment data in tokens.
Using large profile payloads for convenience.
Ignoring stale role or account-status claims.

vi.title: Dữ liệu nhạy cảm trong JWT payload
vi.displayQuestion: Tại sao không nên lưu thông tin nhạy cảm như password hoặc credit card trong JWT payload?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về khả năng đọc payload JWT, data minimization và rủi ro stale claim.
guidance: Giải thích payload của signed JWT đọc được.
Tách integrity khỏi confidentiality.
Nói về log, browser storage, telemetry và screenshot như bề mặt rò rỉ.
Khuyến nghị claims tối thiểu và lookup phía server cho dữ liệu nhạy cảm hoặc hay thay đổi.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ signed nghĩa là bí mật.
Đưa password hash hoặc payment data vào token.
Dùng payload profile lớn cho tiện.
Bỏ qua role hoặc account status bị stale.

ja.title: JWT payload の sensitive data
ja.displayQuestion: password や credit card のような sensitive information を JWT payload に保存すべきでないのはなぜですか。
ja.displayIntent: JWT payload visibility、data minimization、stale claim risk を確認する。
guidance: signed JWT payload は読めることを説明する。
integrity と confidentiality を分けて説明する。
logs、browser storage、telemetry、screenshots などの leakage surfaces を扱う。
最小限の claims と、sensitive または変化する data の server-side lookup を推奨する。
commonMistakes: signed なら secret だと考える。
password hash や payment data を token に入れる。
便利さのために大きな profile payload を使う。
role や account status claims の stale risk を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: JWT.io: Introduction to JSON Web Tokens
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT structure and payload readability context.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 8725: JWT Best Current Practices
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT security best-practice framing.

sourceReferences.label: OWASP JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for JWT storage, confidentiality, and sidejacking risk concepts.

intent: Evaluate production secret-management maturity for JWT signing keys, including entropy, storage, rotation, separation, and incident handling.
primaryQuestion: How should JWT_SECRET be protected in production?

expectedSignals.label: Uses a high-entropy secret for HMAC algorithms or a properly protected private key for asymmetric algorithms; never a human-memorable password.
Stores secrets in a secret manager or protected runtime environment, not in source code, Docker images, frontend bundles, logs, tickets, or shared docs.
Injects secrets at runtime through deployment infrastructure with least privilege and audited access.
Uses different secrets or keys per environment and separates access-token signing keys from refresh-token signing keys when both are JWTs.
Controls allowed algorithms and key IDs server-side; does not let token headers choose arbitrary algorithms or keys.
Plans rotation with overlapping key versions, kid or key versioning, short token lifetimes, and a tested rollback/compromise runbook.
Fails fast if required production secrets are missing instead of falling back to a default development secret.
Avoids exposing JWT_SECRET to the browser; clients verify only with public keys when that architecture requires client-side verification.

redFlags: Commits JWT_SECRET to Git or keeps it in a sample .env copied to production.
Uses fallback strings such as super-secret in production.
Reuses the same secret across dev, staging, production, access tokens, and refresh tokens.
Cannot describe rotation without invalidating every user unexpectedly.
Logs secrets or full tokens during debugging.
Uses weak HS256 secrets vulnerable to offline guessing.

scoringHints.description: Covers entropy, secret-manager storage, runtime injection, environment separation, algorithm constraints, rotation, fail-fast behavior, and incident response.
Knows secrets must not be committed and should be in environment/secret manager but misses rotation, key separation, or algorithm constraints.
Treats JWT_SECRET as a normal config value with weak operational controls.
Answer would expose signing keys or make compromise recovery impractical.

followUps.question: How would you rotate JWT signing keys without logging out every active user at once?
What should the app do if JWT_SECRET is missing in production at startup?

en.title: Protecting JWT secrets
en.displayQuestion: How should JWT_SECRET be protected in production?
en.displayIntent: Assess production-grade secret management for JWT signing keys.
guidance: Discuss entropy, secret managers, runtime injection, and least privilege.
Separate environments and token purposes.
Explain algorithm constraints, key IDs, and rotation windows.
Mention fail-fast behavior and compromise runbooks.
commonMistakes: Using default or human-readable secrets.
Committing secrets to source control.
Reusing one key everywhere.
Having no tested rotation plan.

vi.title: Bảo vệ JWT secret
vi.displayQuestion: JWT_SECRET cần được bảo vệ như thế nào trong production?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá secret management cấp production cho JWT signing key.
guidance: Nói về entropy, secret manager, runtime injection và least privilege.
Tách môi trường và mục đích token.
Giải thích algorithm constraint, key id và rotation window.
Nhắc fail-fast behavior và runbook khi compromise.
commonMistakes: Dùng secret mặc định hoặc dễ đoán.
Commit secret vào source control.
Reuse một key cho mọi nơi.
Không có kế hoạch rotation đã test.

ja.title: JWT secret の保護
ja.displayQuestion: production で JWT_SECRET はどのように保護すべきですか。
ja.displayIntent: JWT signing key の production-grade secret management を評価する。
guidance: entropy、secret manager、runtime injection、least privilege を説明する。
environment と token purpose を分離する。
algorithm constraints、key id、rotation window を説明する。
fail-fast behavior と compromise runbook に触れる。
commonMistakes: default または推測しやすい secret を使う。
secret を source control に commit する。
1 つの key をすべてで再利用する。
tested rotation plan がない。

sourceReferences.label: RFC 8725: JWT Best Current Practices
sourceReferences.note: Used for weak symmetric key and algorithm confusion risk framing.

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Secrets Management Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for secret lifecycle, access control, auditing, and rotation principles.

sourceReferences.label: jsonwebtoken package README
sourceReferences.note: Used for signing key options and token verification behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands Passport.js as authentication middleware with strategy-based integration rather than a complete auth product.
primaryQuestion: What is Passport.js, and what problem does it solve in Express?

expectedSignals.label: Defines Passport.js as authentication middleware for Node.js/Express.
Explains the strategy pattern: LocalStrategy, JwtStrategy, OAuth/OpenID Connect strategies, SAML, GitHub, Google, and other provider-specific strategies.
Explains that Passport standardizes credential extraction, verification callback flow, failure handling, and attaching authenticated identity to req.user.
Mentions session support through serializeUser and deserializeUser when using login sessions.
Mentions stateless JWT usage typically disables sessions for the JWT strategy and validates tokens per request.
Clarifies that Passport does not store users, hash passwords, issue tokens, design roles, or replace authorization policies by itself.
Mentions benefits such as less duplicated auth glue, consistent middleware composition, and integration with many identity providers.

redFlags: Says Passport is an ORM or user database.
Says Passport automatically makes authentication secure without strategy configuration.
Cannot explain what a strategy does.
Mixes session serialization and JWT stateless validation without understanding the difference.
Assumes req.user exists without a successful authenticate middleware.

scoringHints.description: Explains Passport middleware, strategies, req.user, session serialization, JWT stateless usage, and boundaries versus user storage/token issuance/authorization.
Understands strategy-based authentication but misses one boundary such as authorization or session serialization.
Has used Passport snippets but cannot explain the abstraction it provides.
Answer misrepresents Passport's responsibility or would produce confused auth architecture.

followUps.question: What is the difference between using Passport with sessions and using Passport with a JWT strategy?
followUps.purpose: Check stateful versus stateless Passport integration.

followUps.question: After passport.authenticate succeeds, where does the authenticated user information normally appear?

en.title: Passport.js role in Express
en.displayQuestion: What is Passport.js, and what problem does it solve in Express?
en.displayIntent: Check understanding of Passport as strategy-based authentication middleware and its boundaries.
guidance: Define Passport as authentication middleware.
Explain strategies and req.user.
Compare session-based and JWT-based Passport usage.
Clarify what Passport does not do: user storage, password hashing, token policy, and authorization.
commonMistakes: Treating Passport as a complete auth system.
Not understanding strategies.
Confusing sessions with stateless JWT.
Assuming authorization is solved automatically.

vi.title: Vai trò của Passport.js trong Express
vi.displayQuestion: Passport.js là gì và nó giải quyết vấn đề gì trong Express?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về Passport như middleware authentication dựa trên strategy và ranh giới trách nhiệm của nó.
guidance: Định nghĩa Passport là authentication middleware.
Giải thích strategy và req.user.
So sánh cách dùng Passport với session và với JWT.
Làm rõ Passport không tự làm user storage, password hashing, token policy và authorization.
commonMistakes: Xem Passport là hệ thống auth hoàn chỉnh.
Không hiểu strategy là gì.
Nhầm session với JWT stateless.
Tưởng authorization được giải quyết tự động.

ja.title: Express における Passport.js の役割
ja.displayQuestion: Passport.js とは何で、Express でどの問題を解決しますか。
ja.displayIntent: Passport を strategy-based authentication middleware として理解し、その責務の境界を確認する。
guidance: Passport を authentication middleware として定義する。
strategies と req.user を説明する。
session-based と JWT-based の Passport 利用を比較する。
Passport が user storage、password hashing、token policy、authorization を自動で行わないことを明確にする。
commonMistakes: Passport を完全な auth system として扱う。
strategy を理解していない。
session と stateless JWT を混同する。
authorization が自動で解決されると考える。

sourceReferences.label: Passport.js Documentation
sourceReferences.note: Used for Passport definition as Node.js authentication and authorization middleware.

sourceReferences.label: Passport.js: Sessions
sourceReferences.note: Used for serializeUser, deserializeUser, passport.session, and req.user session flow.

sourceReferences.label: Passport.js Strategies
sourceReferences.note: Used for the strategy ecosystem and provider integration framing.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can implement logout and revocation in JWT systems despite stateless access tokens, using realistic token lifetime and server-side control strategies.
primaryQuestion: How do you implement logout when using JWT, given that JWTs cannot be revoked by default?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that a purely stateless signed access token remains valid until exp if the resource server accepts it and no revocation check exists.
Implements client-side logout by deleting in-memory tokens, local storage entries, or clearing auth cookies, but explains this alone does not invalidate already issued access tokens.
Uses short-lived access tokens so the residual window after logout is small.
Revokes refresh tokens on logout by deleting or marking a server-side refresh token record, token family, session record, or device session as revoked.
Uses refresh token rotation and reuse detection so stolen refresh tokens cannot silently mint access tokens after logout.
For high-risk systems, adds a denylist keyed by jti until access token expiry, a user tokenVersion/sessionVersion claim, or a server-side session introspection check.
Supports logout all devices by revoking all refresh token families or incrementing a user/session version.
Clears HttpOnly cookies with the correct path, domain, SameSite, Secure, and expiry/maxAge settings when cookies are used.
Acknowledges trade-offs: denylists and introspection add state and lookup cost, while short TTLs improve statelessness but may require robust refresh UX.

redFlags: Says logout is impossible or unnecessary with JWT.
Only removes the token from the frontend and claims the server token is revoked.
Uses long-lived access tokens with no denylist, refresh token revocation, or version check.
Stores refresh tokens client-side without server-side tracking.
Does not handle logout from all devices or account compromise scenarios.
Clears the wrong cookie name/path/domain and leaves the real auth cookie in place.

scoringHints.description: Explains stateless access-token limits, short TTLs, refresh revocation, rotation/reuse detection, optional access denylist/versioning, cookie clearing, logout-all, and trade-offs.
Handles refresh token revocation and short access tokens but misses one advanced case such as denylist, logout-all, or cookie clearing detail.
Knows to delete tokens on the client but cannot reason about server-side revocation.
Answer leaves long-lived credentials usable after logout or account compromise.

followUps.question: When would you add an access-token denylist even though it makes JWT auth stateful?
How would you implement logout from all devices with refresh token rotation?

en.title: JWT logout and revocation
en.displayQuestion: How do you implement logout when using JWT, given that JWTs cannot be revoked by default?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate practical logout, revocation, and compromise handling in JWT systems.
guidance: Explain why stateless access tokens remain valid until expiry.
Use short-lived access tokens and server-side refresh token revocation.
Discuss rotation, reuse detection, denylists, token versions, and logout all devices.
Include correct cookie clearing when tokens are stored in cookies.
commonMistakes: Claiming client deletion revokes server trust.
Using long-lived access tokens with no revocation control.
Not tracking refresh tokens server-side.
Ignoring logout-all and compromise scenarios.

vi.title: Logout và revocation với JWT
vi.displayQuestion: Làm thế nào để implement logout khi dùng JWT, trong khi JWT không thể revoke mặc định?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá cách xử lý logout, revocation và account compromise trong hệ thống JWT thực tế.
guidance: Giải thích vì sao stateless access token vẫn valid tới khi hết hạn.
Dùng access token ngắn hạn và revoke refresh token phía server.
Nói về rotation, reuse detection, denylist, token version và logout all devices.
Bao gồm cách clear cookie đúng khi token nằm trong cookie.
commonMistakes: Khẳng định xóa token phía client là revoke server trust.
Dùng access token sống lâu mà không có control revoke.
Không track refresh token phía server.
Bỏ qua logout-all và account compromise.

ja.title: JWT logout と revocation
ja.displayQuestion: JWT は default では revoke できない前提で、JWT を使う場合の logout はどう実装しますか。
ja.displayIntent: JWT system における practical logout、revocation、compromise handling を評価する。
guidance: stateless access token が expiry まで valid である理由を説明する。
short-lived access token と server-side refresh token revocation を使う。
rotation、reuse detection、denylist、token version、logout all devices を説明する。
cookie に token を置く場合の正しい cookie clearing を含める。
commonMistakes: client deletion が server trust を revoke すると主張する。
revocation control なしで長寿命 access token を使う。
server-side で refresh token を track しない。
logout-all や compromise scenario を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: OWASP Session Management Cheat Sheet
sourceReferences.note: Used for logout, session invalidation, idle timeout, and lifecycle logging concepts.

sourceReferences.label: RFC 6749: Refreshing an Access Token
sourceReferences.note: Used for refresh token renewal, rotation, and revocation context.

sourceReferences.label: jsonwebtoken package README
sourceReferences.note: Used for exp and jti claim behavior in Node.js JWT implementations.
